title: HIDS-RPL: A Hybrid Deep Learning-Based Intrusion Detection System for RPL in Internet of Medical Things Network

HIDS-RPL: A Hybrid Deep Learning-Based
Intrusion Detection System for RPL in Internet
of Medical Things Network
ABDELWAHED BERGUIGA
 1, AHLEM HARCHAY 1, AND AYMAN MASSAOUDI
 2
1Department of Computer Science, College of Computer and Information Sciences, Jouf University, Sakaka 72388, Saudi Arabia
2Mediatron Laboratory, Higher School of Communications, University of Carthage, Ariana 1054, Tunisia
Corresponding author: Abdelwahed Berguiga (awberguiga@ju.edu.sa)
This work was funded by the Deanship of Graduate Studies and Scientific Research at Jouf University under grant
No. (DGSSR-2023-02-02185).
ABSTRACT The proliferation of the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) has improved medical examination
behaviors in their accuracy, real-time capability, interconnection, and intelligence level. However, the
increasing connectivity of medical devices has also made IoMT networks vulnerable to various cyberattacks,
particularly Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks. Existing intrusion detection systems (IDS) are not
adequately equipped to handle and mitigate these sophisticated cyberattacks. Moreover, given the dynamic
nature of IoMT traffic, where the Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (RPL) is often
deployed to adapt changes in network topology, the intrusion detection system faces significant challenges
in maintaining up-to-date threat detection capabilities. This paper proposes a hybrid Deep Learning-Based
Intrusion Detection System for the RPL protocol in IoMT networks. The suggested model, designated HIDSRPL, results from the hybridization of the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for feature extraction and
the Long Short Term Memory neural network (LSTM), typically employed for sequence data prediction.
We evaluate the proposed algorithm to detect intrusions using the benchmark CIC-DDoS2019 dataset. This
dataset contains a large and diverse collection of traffic flows from real-world DDoS attacks, including SYN
floods, UDP floods, HTTP floods, etc. We evaluated our novel methodology against five DDoS attacks:
DNS, UDP , UDP-Lag, NTP , and SYN. In comparison to the most recent methods, our suggested model
achieves an accuracy of 99.87%, a precision of 98.5%, a recall rate of 98.64%, and an F1-score of 98.54%.
INDEX TERMS Deep learning (DL), intrusion detection system (IDS), routing protocol for low-power and
lossy networks (RPL), Internet of Medical Things (IoMT), convolutional neural network (CNN), long short
term memory neural network (LSTM), DDoS attacks.
I. INTRODUCTION
The incorporation of medical devices into the Internet of
Things (IoT), also known as Healthcare 5.0, has given rise
to the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) in the healthcare
industry. This innovation has opened the way for a new
era of patient care and medical practice. IoMT denotes
the merging of advanced technology and has a crucial
function in enhancing medical procedures, optimizing patient
results, and transforming healthcare services. This large and
The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and
approving it for publication was Xianzhi Wang
.
complicated ecosystem consists of several sophisticated medical devices and sensors [1], [2], each meticulously designed
to monitor, gather, and communicate crucial healthcare data
instantly. The IoMT involves an extensive variety of devices,
from wearable health gadgets such as fitness trackers and
smartwatches that survey vital signs and physical activity to
implanted medical equipment such as pacemakers and insulin
pumps that deliver life-saving treatments [3], [4], [5].
The Internet of Medical Things operates via the seamless
exchange of data, and one of its main objectives is addressing
the issue of establishing efficient and reliable connectivity
between these varied devices. RPL is a crucial facilitator

A. Berguiga et al.: HIDS-RPL: A Hybrid DL-Based IDS for RPL in IoMT Network
for the IoMT ecosystem. Indeed, RPL’s innovative design
for low-power and resource-constrained devices makes it
the ideal routing protocol for IoMT applications [6], [7],
[8]. Furthermore, it demonstrates exceptional abilities in
optimizing power consumption, which is a significant issue
to address in the field of IoMT, where the duration of battery
life can impose restrictions.
Indeed, for IoT networks, RPL is deemed the de facto
routing protocol, and its adaptive routing capabilities allow
IoMT networks to operate seamlessly even as network
topologies evolve. RPL ensures efficient routing of data,
whether in a hospital setting where wearable devices must
relay patient data swiftly to central monitoring systems or
in remote patient monitoring scenarios where data from
implantable devices must traverse long distances.
However, the expanding development and influence of the
IoMT in the healthcare industry have rendered it a potential
objective for cyberattacks. The IoMT devices are designed
with constrained computational resources, rigid power consumption, and often limited memory capacity. Consequently,
these devices do not possess the capability to execute complex
security algorithms, rendering them susceptible and mainly
vulnerable against a range of security threats, including
data integrity attacks, denial-of-service attacks, interception,
and information leaking. These potential consequences
may encompass inaccurate diagnosis, postponed treatment,
or possibly grave repercussions, including death.
In this context, the IDS is crucial for strengthening
the protection of the IoMT against malicious attacks. IDS
is an intelligent safety system that constantly analyzes
network traffic, meticulously examining patterns and unusual
behaviors to identify any suspicious or malicious activity.
Furthermore, an IDS system should be capable of mitigating
the potential impacts of intrusions before they compromise
IoMT device security and malfunction [9], [10], [11], [12].
The key goal of this study is to propose a novel deep
learning-powered intrusion detection system for RPL in medical IoT networks (HIDS-RPL). This was accomplished by
employing a hybrid methodology that involves a combination
of the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and the Long
Short Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks. Considering
the excellent accuracy and resemblance to real attacks in
the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset, we trained and evaluated the
HIDS-RPL on it. HIDS-RPL primarily focuses on IoMT
devices, but its fundamental principles and structure can be
used for various IoT applications like smart cities, industrial
IoT, and connected home environments. HIDS-RPL is an
adaptable solution for securing different IoT ecosystems
beyond healthcare due to its capability to identify and react
to a wide spectrum of cyber threats [13].
Additionally, the integration of the RPL protocol in our
research provides a crucial function in the transmission
of data between IoMT nodes, which directly affects the
efficiency of intrusion detection approaches. Recent IDS
studies commonly evaluate performance without thoroughly
examining the details of the routing protocol used for
transmitting data across IoMT nodes. The choice of a
routing protocol can substantially influence the network
traffic patterns and, consequently, the effectiveness of IDS
systems. By explicitly including the factor of RPL in our
study, we enhance the accuracy of our evaluation of the
IDS performance in IoT contexts. This methodology allows
us to gain better comprehension of how different traffic
patterns, governed by the routing protocol, influence the
Intrusion Detection System’s capabilities. To summarize, this
work presents three key innovations: (1) RPL-aware feature
engineering targeting control-plane threats, including DIO
interval anomalies and Flooding attacks; (2) a fog-tiered
computational architecture engineered to reduce processing
demands in Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) ecosystems; and (3) HIDS-RPL, a lightweight specification-based
intrusion detection system customized for IoMT networks.
Evaluated against the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset, the framework
demonstrates robust performance with 99.87% accuracy,
98.5% precision, 98.64% recall, and a 98.54% F1-score.
The remainder of the paper is carried out as follows:
Section II gives an overview of the RPL routing protocol
and attacks in RPL-based networks. Section III outlines the
related work regarding the relevant scope of attack detection
in RPL-based IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area
Networks (6LoWPAN) in IoT networks. Section IV provides
the methodology and detailed techniques that are used to
build our proposed intrusion detection system. Section V
contains the experimental setup and presents the performance
evaluation and comparative study of our proposed scheme
with classical CNN and LSTM models. Finally, in SectionVI,
we conclude the research paper and envision our future
directions.
II. OVERVIEW OF RPL ROUTING PROTOCOL
The routing protocol RPL is proposed by the Routing Over
Low-power and Lossy networks (ROLL) working group
and adopted as an Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)
standard in the Request for Comments (RFC) 6550 [14] for
low-power and lossy networks. RPL exploits the concept of
directed acyclic graphs (DAG) to reduce access costs between
sender and receiver via a hierarchical structure.
A. TOPOLOGY CONSTRUCTION
RPL constructs destination-oriented DAGs (DODAGs) to
form loop-free topologies, structuring nodes into a hierarchy
with a root, children, and descendants. The construction of a
DODAG is carried out using Objective Functions (OF) and a
collection of metric parameters to establish the ‘‘best path’’
between any two nodes in the created topology [15]. This
method allows RPL to create one or more logical routing
topologies (DODAG) on an existing physical infrastructure.
Every RPL instance possesses a unique ID, known as
RPLInstanceID, and operates independently from its peers.
In a given RPL instance, a node can belong to at most one
DODAG to avoid routing loops. Figure1 illustrates a scenario
of two RPL instances having three DODAGs. Node 10 is

FIGURE 1. Structure of an RPL instance with three DODAGs.
part of several RPL instances (instance 1 and instance 2);
however, it has to be integrated into a DODAG inside each
instance in order to preserve topology integrity and prevent
routing loops. RPL uses four distinct identifiers, namely
DODAGID, DODAGV ersionNumber, RPLInstanceID, and
Rank, to prevent routing loops and ensure network topology
consistency:
• DODAGID (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic
Graph Identifier): This identifier is used to uniquely
identify a DODAG within the scope of one RPL
instance. All other nodes in the DODAG derive their
DODAGID from the root node’s DODAGID.
• DODAGV ersionNumber: This identifier is used to
specify the current version of the DODAG and to
track changes to the DODAG topology. Each time the
DODAG topology changes, its sequential counter is
raised.
• RPLInstanceID: This identifier is used to distinguish
between different instances of RPL running on the same
network. In other words, a single low-power network
may contain one or more DODAGs that come together
to form an RPL instance identifiable through its unique
RPLInstanceID.
• Rank: Each node within the Destination Oriented DAG
(DODAG) is assigned a rank that establishes its specific
placement in relation to other nodes and to the DODAG
root. It is imperative that each node possesses a rank
greater than its immediate parent. The determination of
the rank is allocated to the Objective Function, which
takes into account the parent node’s rank and metric to
determine the node’s own rank and assess whether a
change of parent node is warranted or not [14]. The rank
value ascends in the downward direction and descends
in the upward direction. In some cases, the rank may
correspond to a straightforward hop-count distance from
the root.
To establish and manage destination-oriented DAG (DODAG)
upward routes, the RPL specification outlines four distinct
types of control messages, namely DODAG Information
Object (DIO), DODAG Information Solicitation (DIS),
Destination Advertisement Object (DAO), and Destination Advertisement Object Acknowledgment (DAO-ACK).
As stated in [14], these messages are categorized as a subtype
of the ICMPv6 protocol, with a specific type value of 155.
• DODAG Information Object (DIO): DIO messages
are used by the root node of the DODAG to advertise its presence and to provide other nodes with
the information they need to join the DODAG. Its
essential role is to carry relevant information and
configuration parameters, for example, the DODAG
identifier (DODAGID) and the DODAG version number
(DODAGV ersionNumber).
• DODAG Information Solicitation (DIS): DIS messages are used by nodes to request DIO messages

A. Berguiga et al.: HIDS-RPL: A Hybrid DL-Based IDS for RPL in IoMT Network
from the root node of the DODAG. DIS messages are
typically transmitted by nodes that are newly joining
the network or by nodes that have lost their connection
to the root node. Upon the reception of a DIS, any
other node within range has the ability to respond by
transmitting a DODAG Information Object (DIO) that
includes the DODAG configuration option. In doing
so, the new node is able to evaluate and select the
most appropriate parent and subsequently become a
member of its corresponding DODAG. This process
occurs initially during the protocol’s initiation phase
and subsequently whenever a node desires to integrate
a DODAG.
• Destination Advertisement Object (DAO):RPL nodes
(except the DODAG root) periodically transmit DAO
messages to their parents to advertise their reachability
to other nodes in the DODAG. DAO messages also
contain information about the node’s rank and the route
to the destination.
• Destination Advertisement Object Acknowledgment
(DAO-ACK): DAO-ACK messages are used by nodes
to acknowledge receipt of DAO messages. DAO-ACK
messages are also used to inform the sender of the DAO
message about the route to the destination.
B. ATTACKS IN RPL-BASED NETWORKS
This subsection presents an analysis of potential attacks in
RPL networks, emphasizing key features that the IDS must
detect:
• Flooding attack: This refers to launching a large
amount of traffic to saturate the network and make it
unavailable to legitimate users. Within the context of
RPL, a malicious node can initiate a flooding attack
through the transmission of a substantial number of DIS
(DODAG Information Solicitation) control messages to
its neighbors [16], [17], [18]. This causes the neighboring nodes to generate more traffic by broadcasting DIO
(DODAG Information Object) messages. Indeed, the
sudden increase in the number of DIS control messages
could indicate that it is being targeted by a flooding
attack. Additionally, the quantity of broadcast DIO
messages dispatched by individual nodes in response
to DIS control messages results in a notable elevation
of the end-to-end delay, which may indicate network
congestion as a consequence of a flooding attack.
• Wormhole attack: The primary objective of this attack
is to establish a communication tunnel connecting two
malicious nodes, enabling the unauthorized relaying of
network traffic across distinct segments of the network.
This can allow the attackers to eavesdrop on or tamper
with traffic or to launch other attacks [19], [20]. Initially,
the attackers (usually two or more malicious nodes)
begin by creating a communication tunnel between
them. This tunnel can be established by leveraging
another network interface, which may employ either
wired or wireless links in accordance with the network’s
specific infrastructure. Once the tunnel is successfully
created, the attacker can intercept the DIO messages
transmitted by the legitimate nodes through the regular communication link, subsequently replaying them
through the alternative network interface. These DIO
messages contain critical information about network
topology and routes.
Upon receipt of the replayed DIO messages, the
receiving attacker node processes them through its
secondary interface and designates the sender (i.e.,
the malicious attacker node) as its preferred parent.
Consequently, the malicious node is integrated into the
routing information as a desired parent, resulting in
all regular traffic received by the initial attacker on
its primary interface being replicated and transmitted
through the second interface. This manipulated route
can disrupt the network communication and allow
the attackers to intercept and manipulate data traffic. Wormhole attacks can seriously compromise the
integrity and efficiency of a network using the RPL
protocol. Detecting and mitigating such attacks typically
involves the use of secure authentication mechanisms,
intrusion detection systems, and careful monitoring of
the network topology. Therefore, we can train deep
learning models to detect and prevent flooding attacks
in RPL in real time.
• Mirai botnet attack: The infamous Mirai botnet
employs a variety of Distributed Denial of Service
(DDoS) attack methods, encompassing UDP flooding,
DNS flooding, VSE flooding, TCP flooding, HTTP
flooding, and GRE flooding [21], [22], [23]. In October
2016, the Mirai botnet exploited the insecure configuration of many Internet of Things (IoT) devices and
launched a series of massive DDoS attacks against Dyn,
a major DNS provider, various game servers, telecom
companies, anti-DDoS providers, and other seemingly
unrelated websites. The Mirai botnet’s enormous volume of traffic caused Dyn’s DNS servers to crash,
leading to widespread disruption to affected websites.
The attack reached a peak rate of over 1 Tbps, causing
significant economic damage. Mirai exploited security
vulnerabilities in IoT devices, such as weak passwords
and outdated software, and converted these devices into
bots [24]. Then, these bots initiate DDoS attacks against
targeted websites and networks. In the healthcare sector,
Mirai-based DDoS attacks pose a significant threat to
patient care. For example, a Mirai-based DDoS attack
on a hospital’s patient monitoring system could disrupt
the monitoring of critically infirm patients, leading to
adverse health outcomes.
• DIS attack: The DIS packet attack is a form of
DoS attack that exploits the DIS protocol within RPL
networks. In this attack, a malicious node floods the
compromised node with a significant volume of DIS
control messages. When the legitimate node receives
a DIS message from a neighboring intruder node, it is

obligated to reset its DIO Trickle timer and subsequently
responds with a DIO message [25], [26], [27]. Such
actions consume considerable amounts of the limited
battery energy available at the legitimate node and
can potentially result in network congestion. The DIS
attack shares similarities, both in characteristics and
impact, with the Hello Flood attack within wireless
sensor networks. Furthermore, the DIS attack exploits
DIS control messages to adversely affect network
performance, increase CPU usage, or deplete the battery
resources of the targeted node [28].
• Ranking attack: The DODAG version number is a
variable used to maintain DODAG topology consistency.
Once the nodes receive a version number exceeding
that of the root node, they must update their DODAG
information. Similarly, if the root node receives a version
number higher than expected, it initiates a global repair
to rebuild the entire topology. The version number
attack exploits this mechanism to disrupt the network by
sending a higher version number to neighboring nodes.
By exploiting this vulnerability, the attacker disrupts
the network by transmitting a higher version number
to neighboring nodes [29]. Therefore, this manipulation
causes the root node to initiate a global repair, disrupting
the normal operation of the network and potentially
compromising its overall functionality. Additionally,
this forces all other nodes in the network to initiate a
global repair, which consumes more resources and can
lead to the formation of loops in the topology, further
depleting node resources.
III. RELATED WORKS
In [30] V arghese et al. present a novel Intrusion Detection System (IDS) framework. This system leverages a
combination of statistical and machine learning techniques,
constituting an anomaly detection mechanism capable of
discerning abnormal patterns indicative of DDoS activities.
By capitalizing on the inherent programmability of SDN, the
proposed IDS exhibits a remarkable degree of adaptability
to dynamic network conditions. Authors underscore the
critical importance of swift detection and robust response
to DDoS threats, highlighting their potential to disrupt
network availability. The framework’s efficiency in detecting
and mitigating various DDoS attacks has been further
demonstrated using simulated scenarios.
The authors in [31] considered the IDS systems to
detect DDoS attacks. The study surveys a range of existing
approaches, tracing their adaptation to the increasingly
dynamic nature of DDoS threats. The authors discussed various detection strategies, such as anomaly-based, signaturebased, and hybrid methodologies, and then they analyzed
their strengths and limitations. By evaluating and comparing
different detection strategies, the review contributes insights
into the diverse landscape of IDS mechanisms, aiding
researchers in making informed decisions when devising
resilient defenses against the wide spectrum of DDoS attacks.
The research proposed by Ines et al. [32] focuses on the
application of deep autoencoders in Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) for Industrial Control Systems (ICS).
The study outlines the challenges posed by DDoS threats to
ICS, which are critical infrastructures. The proposed NIDS
leverages deep autoencoders to capture complicated patterns
in network traffic and discriminate anomalies indicative of
DDoS activities. The autoencoder model is trained on normal
network behavior to learn a representative encoding, and
deviations from this learned pattern trigger alerts for potential
DDoS incidents. Through experimentation and evaluation,
the authors proved that the studied NIDS is efficient in
detecting DDoS threats. Furthermore, the utilization of deep
autoencoders demonstrates the ability to adapt to the complexity of ICS network traffic, thereby offering a promising
approach for strengthening cybersecurity measures in critical
infrastructures.
In [33], the authors considered IoT systems and presented
a comprehensive literature review on attacks and defense
strategies in RPL-based IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless
Personal Area Networks (6LoWPAN). Indeed, the study
examines the landscape of various attack vectors, including
routing attacks, power depletion attacks, and malicious
node intrusions, highlighting the diversity of vulnerabilities
inherent in these networks. Additionally, their work takes
an in-depth look at existing defense mechanisms designed
to mitigate these threats. These mechanisms encompass
cryptographic protocols, secure routing protocols, anomaly
detection techniques, and trust-based solutions. Additionally,
the study emphasizes how crucial proactive security measures
are in IoT environments, given the increasing amount of
connected things and the potential implications of security
breaches. By synthesizing findings based on a variety of
research studies, the analysis offered a thorough insight into
the evolving threat landscape and corresponding defensive
strategies used to build resilience in an RPL-based 6LoWPAN
network.
Shadi et al. [34] considered a passive rule-based approach
for detecting sinkhole attacks in RPL. Indeed, this type
of attack represents a significant security threat wherein
malicious nodes attract legitimate traffic in order to disrupt
network operations and therefore compromise data integrity.
In this regard, the proposed approach aims to mitigate
such attacks by utilizing passive monitoring techniques and
rule-based analysis to identify anomalous behavior indicative
of sinkhole activity. The methodology of the proposed
scheme involves the establishment of predefined rules based
on network parameters and behaviors characteristic of
normal network operation. These rules are derived from
an understanding of the expected communication patterns
within RPL-based IoT networks. Then, when deviations
from these established norms are detected, alerts are
triggered to indicate the potential presence of a sinkhole
attack. Experimental performance evaluation of their work
demonstrates the passive rule-based technique is efficient in
detecting sinkhole attacks while minimizing false positives.

In addition to this, the approach offers a proactive means
of safeguarding RPL-based IoT networks against sinkhole
attacks, consequently improving the entire security posture
of IoT deployments.
The researchers in [35] focused on detecting Hello Flooding attacks targeting the RPL protocol within IoT networks
by employing various machine learning algorithms. Hello
Flooding attacks present a significant risk to the RPL-based
IoT networks, where malicious nodes flood the network with
excessive hello messages, causing network congestion and
performance degradation. The authors suggested that the use
of multiple machine learning (ML) algorithms, including
support vector machines, decision trees, and k-nearest
neighbors, is able to predict Hello Flooding attacks based on
the behavior of the network.
Moreover, by training these algorithms on features
extracted from network traffic data, such as packet transmission rates and routing table updates, the detection system can
accurately identify any abnormal activities associated with
Hello Flooding attacks. According to the performance study,
the use of machine learning algorithms can bring significant
advantages to both understanding and predicting Hello
Flooding attacks and consequently demonstrated substantial
improvements in minimizing false positives.
The work [36] presents a deep learning (DL) method
to detect intrusion in WSNs. Indeed, the convolutional
neural networks (CNNs) architecture is deployed to examine
network traffic patterns and identify intrusion attempts. The
CNN model is trained and supervised on labeled datasets
for detecting normal and malicious network traffic. However,
in the context of WSNs, obtaining sufficiently labeled
datasets may be challenging due to the restricted resources
and the limited characteristics of these networks. Additionally, CNNs are computationally intensive and require
significant processing power and memory resources for
training and inference. This can lead to increased overhead
and potentially impact the network’s overall effectiveness and
performance.
Alsulami et al. [37] present a new automated and intelligent
IDS to detect and classify malicious activities in IoT
networks. The proposed IDS can classify network traffic
into five categories: normal, Mirai attack, Denial-of-Service
(DoS) attack, Scan attack, and man-in-the-middle (MITM)
attack. Furthermore, the authors studied five supervised
learning models, including shallow neural networks (SNNs),
decision trees (DT), bagged trees (BT), support vector
machine (SVM), and k-nearest neighbors (kNN). The authors
evaluated these models using the IoTID20 dataset, achieving
accuracy ranging from 99.40% to 100%.
In reference [38], Al-Haija et al. provide an intelligent,
self-reliant system for detecting and classifying cyberattacks
in IoT networks using a deep CNN. The proposed system
comprises three subsystems: Feature Engineering, Feature
Learning, and Traffic Classification. Each sub-system is
designed to efficiently process, learn, and classify network
traffic data. The model achieved high classification accuracy
rates of over 99.3% for binary classification traffic and
98.2% for multi-class classification, outperforming existing
methods in handling IoT-related cyber threats.
Researchers within [39] developed and evaluated an intelligent system for detecting Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks
in web applications through ML approaches, specifically
optimizable k-nearest neighbors, optimizable naïve Bayes,
and hybrid ensemble learning of decision trees. The XSSAttacks-2019 dataset, which contains both benign and XSS
attack traffic, was employed to justify the system’s effectiveness. Evaluation metrics, such as confusion matrix analysis,
detection accuracy, precision, sensitivity, harmonic means,
and detection time, were utilized. The hybrid learning-based
model demonstrated superior performance, achieving 99.8%
accuracy, precision, and sensitivity with a detection time
of 103.1 µSec, surpassing numerous current XSS detection
systems.
In [40], the authors introduced a lightweight double-stage
framework for detecting malicious DNS over HTTPS (DoH)
traffic through a hybrid learning methodology. The architecture consists of two sequential layers: the first utilizes
random fine trees (RF) and detects them as DoH traffic or
non-DoH traffic, and the second employs Adaboost trees
(ADT) to further classify DoH traffic into benign or malicious
categories. The system’s effectiveness is optimized by using
only six out of thirty-three available features, identified via
principal component analysis (PCA), and by implementing
random under-sampling (RUS) to decrease the sample size.
Experimental evaluations revealed a high-performing scheme
with layer one (resp. layer two) identification prediction
overheads of 0.83 µSec (resp. 2.27 µSec) and predicted
accuracy of 99.4% (resp. 100%).
The authors in [41] addressed the security concerns across
several architectural layers, including the application, cloud,
network, data, and physical layers. The authors explained
layer-based security requirements and examined potential
vulnerabilities and attacks against IoT devices and classified
IoT attacks. Authors conclude that even if an organization has
implemented a typical security system, it will be virtually
impractical to protect against a DDoS attack due to the
simultaneous occurrence of many attacks.
Altunay et al. [42] analyze anomaly-based IDSs implemented using convolutional neural networks, autoencoders,
deep belief networks, long short-term memory networks, and
diverse combinations of these techniques within supervisory
control and data acquisition (SCADA) networks as reported
in the literature. The research evaluates the efficacy of
these approaches in detecting cyberattacks, highlighting
their strengths and limitations based on their performance
outcomes.
In [43], the authors proposed an adaptive protocol that
combines server-based and direct access protocols. The
working principle of the proposed protocol is detailed,
and its functionality is compared to MQTT using OPNET

simulations and real-world environments. Evaluation performance indicates that the proposed protocol maintains
functionality even during server failures and demonstrates its
efficiency in bandwidth utilization. While it showed reduced
latency in the direct communication mode, the proposed
adaptive protocol experienced slightly higher latency in
server-based communication. However, it remained fully
operational in both scenarios, highlighting its robustness and
adaptability in various network conditions.
Researchers within [44] developed an algorithm, namely
TB-MFCC, for data augmentation, feature extraction, and
classification. The studied TB-MFCC multifuse feature integrates data augmentation and feature extraction processes.
The Multi-stacked Convolutional Neural Network (MCNN)
with Attention-based Bidirectional Long Short Term Memory
(A-BiLSTM) are employed to capture complex nonlinear
relationships among features. Results demonstrate that the
studied approach attains an accuracy of 98.66%, decreases
the False Positive Rate (FPR) by 1.01%, and minimizes
loss to 0%. However, the main drawback of the suggested
TB-MFCC Multifuse Feature is the considerable processing
time it necessitates.
The authors in[45] propose a deep neural network-statistical
differential privacy (DNN-SDP) scheme to protect individuals’ private and sensitive data. The authors apply statistical
methods such as weight of evidence and information value
to generate initially perturbed data. This data is then
processed using a Laplace computation-based differential
privacy mechanism to produce the final perturbed dataset.
Experiments are conducted using the Census income, bank
marketing, and heart disease datasets. Compared with other
works in the literature, the proposed DNN-SDP algorithm
exhibits a classification accuracy of 97.4%, a precision of
98.2%, a recall rate of 99%, and an F-measure of 98.7%.
In [46], researchers developed a blockchain system that
uses homomorphic encryption to ensure safe search and
keyword-based database access. The suggested solution
offers a secure key revocation mechanism and modifies
certain policies accordingly. The proposed framework incorporates blockchain and trust chain technologies to address the
efficiency and security challenges present in existing digital
healthcare data sharing systems. Compared to benchmark
models, the proposed framework demonstrates superior
security and a more effective searching mechanism for the
healthcare system, providing a foundation for integrating
blockchain into trust-centric medical data management
platforms.
Ismail et al. [47] proposed a machine learning methodology for the classification and prediction of DDoS attacks. The

IV. PROPOSED METHODOLOGY
The following section details our proposed intrusion detection system scheme. Eventually, the prime goal of an
IDS is its capacity to identify and distinguish various
DDoS security attacks in RPL-based medical IoT networks.
To accomplish this aim, the design should incorporate
multiple cooperative elements and employ adaptable and
flexible techniques to maximize the accuracy of intrusion
detection. Our suggested IDS strategy includes a hybrid
configuration that combines different features, placement
methods, and detection approaches, will be explained in more

several edge devices, fog nodes effectively minimize the

B. DDoS ATTACK MODEL
This section analyzes the security implications of a healthcare
system in which a malicious node seeks to exploit the
communication channel to cause damage to wearable sensor
devices, such as heart rate monitors, breathing rate monitors,
and activity level sensors. Figure 3 depicts the method by
which the attacker infiltrates the healthcare system through
the injection of malevolent control messages that interrupt
or disable healthcare services. Compromised devices employ
multiple techniques to attack fog nodes in the intermediary
layer, such as flooding the network with an overwhelming
number of requests that disrupt its functionality.
A malicious actor can manipulate the DODAG Information Solicitation (DIS) message within the RPL protocol,
frequently utilized for routing updates, in a healthcare system.
A breached device may launch a DIS flooding attack by
producing an excessive quantity of DIS messages, causing
adjacent nodes to continuously reply with DODAG Information Object (DIO) messages. This artificially generated
exchange results in increased network traffic, depleting
important bandwidth and processing resources at the fog
nodes on the intermediary layer. The significant rise in
traffic can substantially delay the network, as these fog
nodes are crucial for processing and transmitting patient data
from wearable sensors. This may result in interruptions or
suspension of real-time monitoring of vital indicators such
as heart rate, respiratory rate, and activity levels.
Furthermore, the continuous flow of DIS requests forces
adjacent nodes to reset their Trickle timings and consistently reply, swiftly exhausting their energy reserves and
seriously compromising the network’s operational stability.
The resulting attack not only elevates end-to-end latency but
also affects the topology by necessitating frequent updates,
disrupting the network, and potentially generating routing
loops. An attack on an IoMT network can significantly impact
the prompt transmission of essential health information,
thereby affecting patient care and safety.
C. CNN-LSTM MODEL DESIGN
The combination of deep learning networks, specifically
convolutional neural networks, and long short-term memory
networks in the context of the internet of medical things can
be emerged as a crucial paradigm in the field of intrusion
detection systems within IoMT. This convergence represents
a significant development that has profound implications for
the security and privacy of IoMT environments, as it enables
the extraction of complex features and data patterns essential
for identifying network intrusions.
CNN and LSTM possess distinct characteristics that make
them well-suited for their respective roles within the CNNLSTM framework. CNN is particularly effective at extracting
features from data. The capacity to learn extensive traffic data
allows CNN to identify useful features that are highly relevant
in large-scale network environments. The structure of a CNN
comprises three primary layers: the convolution layer, the
pooling layer, and the fully connected layer. The convolution
layer focuses on extracting features from the input data, while
the pooling layer samples the extracted features. The fully
connected layer connects the extracted features and utilizes a
classifier to generate accurate classification results.
LSTM, on the other hand, is an enhanced version of
the traditional recurrent neural network (RNN) method. Its
distinct advantage lies in its capacity to collect long-range
dependencies and contextual data, which makes it especially
appropriate for the dynamic and changing characteristics of
IoMT networks. Compared to traditional RNN units, LSTM

D. HIDS-RPL FRAMEWORK
Algorithm 1 illustrates the pseudocode for the HIDS-RPL
procedure. This procedure is executed whenever a DIO, DIS,
or DAO control message is received from a neighboring node.
Firstly, the procedure analyzes the control message and then

executes the appropriate routing management operations.
In this regard, if a control message is received from a neighbor
that is suspected of being an attacker, the packet is discarded
and the alarm procedure is triggered against the suspicious
node. In this research, we have introduced a threshold referred
to as τ , which represents the time interval between the
reception of consecutive control messages that originate from
the same adjacent node. When this interval is equal to or less
than τ , there is suspicion that the neighboring node may have
malicious intentions. Conversely, if the interval exceedsτ , the
neighbor is presumed to be non-malicious. The value of τ is
drawn from the work of Thulasiraman amd Wang [53] and
has been determined to be 500 milliseconds.
When a node receives a control message from a
non-existing node in the neighborhood table, the receiving
node first checks the message type to determine if it is a DIO,
DAO, or DIS message. Then, it adds the 6LoWPAN IP source
node originator of the control message to its neighborhood
table. Afterwards, the node triggers a call to the intrusion
detection system procedure (HIDS-RPL) to check whether
the message is an attack or not. The IDS procedure is invoked
every 30 seconds using the ready variable.
The selection of a 30-second interval for IDS invocation is
grounded in the RIP (Routing Information Protocol) design
principles [54], which harmonize routing update frequency
with overhead in small-to-medium networks. RPL networks
frequently experience topology changes, including nodes
joining, leaving, or modifying routes. Drawing from RIP’s
efficacy in balancing update frequency and overhead in
resource-constrained environments, this interval serves as a
baseline to mitigate energy exhaustion while maintaining
threat detection efficacy. Increasing the frequency of checks
risks exhausting limited battery resources, especially in
sensor-based devices with constrained capacity. An irregular
schedule could compromise network stability. Consequently,
a 30-second interval may adequately detect anomalies in
normal RPL communication while maintaining minimal
overhead in IoMT networks with moderately mobile wireless
sensor nodes. Substantially, this interval is variable and
can be dynamically adjusted according to specific network
requirements and operational conditions. While 30 seconds
functions as a dependable baseline, it may be modified to
suit specific network factors, attack incidents, and device
capabilities. During periods of increased node mobility or
traffic spikes, system engineers may shorten this duration to
facilitate more frequent topology alterations. Conversely, for
reliable, low-traffic deployments, an extended duration can
further reduce energy use. Our suggested solution employs
adaptive strategies to balance resource utilization with precise
and prompt detection, delivering strong performance across
various IoMT environments. The HIDS-RPL procedure
performs the following functions:
1) Initialization and configuration: This process incorporates the establishment and initial configuration
of the adjacent (i.e., neighbor) and malicious (i.e.,
prohibited list) tables.
2) Early identification of malicious nodes: The
HIDS-RPL procedure is designed to quickly discern
the source of received control messages, with a
particular focus on detecting messages originating from
prohibited list nodes. In such a case, the received
message is quickly discarded and a corresponding alert
is triggered.
3) Adjacent table maintenance: Neighbor table maintenance is an integral part of the responsibility of the
HIDS-RPL procedure. This task includes adding new
entries to the neighbor table when new neighboring
nodes are discovered, as well as simultaneously
updating existing entries in the table to reflect changes
in the node’s interactions with its neighbors.
4) Periodic evaluation of malicious neighbors: At regular intervals of 30 seconds, the HIDS-RPL procedure
is executed with the aim of identifying potentially
malicious nodes within the neighboring table. The
procedure uses a set of techniques, including examining inconsistencies within the neighbor table and

V. EXPERIMENTAL SETUP AND RESULTS
This section outlines the experimental setup and methodology
employed to assess the proposed HIDS-RPL framework.
The research employed the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset, which
comprises records of real-world traffic. To fix the imbalance
between the classes, balanced random sampling was used to
make sure that DDoS attacks such as DNS, UDP , UDP-Lag,
NTP , SYN, and benign traffic were all represented the same
way. The preprocessing steps included fixing missing values,
eliminating duplicates, and normalizing the data to improve
the dataset’s consistency. Next, we divided the dataset into
80% for training and 20% for testing to ensure a precise
evaluation. We conducted the studies in Python, utilizing
the TensorFlow and Keras libraries. We evaluate and discuss
the model’s performance across several attack types using
evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and
F1-score.
A. DATASET DESCRIPTION
During this research, the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset, created by
the Canadian Institute for Cybersecurity (CIC), was used to
evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed intrusion detection
system. The dataset includes a broad variety of the most
recent and widespread Distributed Denial-of-Service attacks,
utilizing real data from sources including LDAP , MSSQL,
NetBIOS, PortMap, UDP , SYN, and UDP-Lag. These attacks
can be found in CSV and PCAP files [13], [55], [56]. Table 5
presents the contents of the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset, which
consists of about 50,063,123 records. Of these, 50,006,249
rows are assigned to DDoS attacks, and 56,874 rows are
devoted to benign traffic. There are 88 unique features in each
row, and the dataset is split into two categories: malicious
and benign. Using the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset, we evaluate
the effectiveness of our suggested HIDS-RPL model, paying
particular attention to its capacity to accurately identify and
categorize harmful traffic [57].
1) CLASS IMBALANCE RESOLUTION
With the purpose of addressing the problem of class
imbalance in our dataset, we employed the balanced random sampling technique [58], [59]. This approach offers
the benefit of minimizing class distribution imbalance by
guaranteeing equitable representation of all classes during the
sampling phase. Multiple merits justify the choice to employ
the balanced random sampling method. Firstly, it enhances
the development of a more reliable and consistent model by
providing a sufficient representation of classes that have been
excluded. By employing this approach, the model is capable
of effectively acquiring the unique characteristics of each
category, leading to improved precision in classifying data.
For example, the DNS attack comprises 5,074,414 samples,
while the benign flow type comprises 56,874 samples. The
presence of class imbalances leads to overfitting issues.
Toward this aim, we collected 56,000 traffic samples for
each type of attack. By employing this method, our dataset
successfully obtained an equitable distribution across the
different classes, consisting of a total of 336,000 records, each
defined by 88 features.
Additionally, to ensure the data’s integrity and completeness in our dataset, we systematically substitute all infinite
numbers (np.inf and -np.inf) with NaN and then remove
any rows that have missing values. Next, we have improved
the dataset by removing constant and duplicate rows.
By employing this methodology, our dataset successfully
achieved a total of 312,134 records. As mentioned earlier,
we evaluated our novel methodology against five DDoS
attacks: DNS, UDP , UDP-Lag, NTP , and SYN. We chose
these five types due to their high occurrence rate and
significant threat to system security.

3) DATA PRE-PROCESSING
As stated earlier, the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset is based on
real-world data and includes a wide variety of the most
recent and common DDoS attacks [66]. The dataset has
been subdivided into 5 distinct attack subtypes, thereby
facilitating a more comprehensive and diverse set of attacks
for the purpose of DDoS attack detection. Each record
within the dataset is characterized by 20 features, and
the attack type is classified using a binary classification
approach, distinguishing between normal and malicious
traffic. Features in the collection include timestamps, source
and destination IP addresses, port numbers, packet sizes,
and other characteristics. To further verify the effectiveness
of our proposed model, 80% of the whole database is

framework:
1) Data Collection and Input Layer: The first stage
involves the input layer, which acquires real-time
data from wearable sensors (IoMT devices) via the
data-collecting component. The data is analyzed to
discern properties such as network service types,
network protocol types, network connection time, and
connection status [67], [68].
2) Preprocessing Stage: The collected data undergoes a
sequence of preprocessing steps, including digitization
and normalization, following established data processing procedures. This preprocessing phase prepares
the dataset by eliminating any inconsistencies, ensuring uniformity, standardization, and normalization.
We preprocess the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset similarly
to match the real-time data characteristics, enabling
effective training of the IDS in identifying anomalies
or threats.
3) Feature Extraction with CNN: After preprocessing, the
data is inputted into the convolution layer, where feature extraction is performed using a one-dimensional
convolution operation. Every convolution layer is
followed with a pooling layer, which decreases the
dimensionality of features to improve convergence,
eliminate unnecessary information, and avoid overfitting. The fully linked layer combines these local
elements to create an accurate representation. The
fully connected layer applies the Rectified Linear
Unit (ReLU) activation function to enhance learning
capacities and prevent problems like disappearing
gradients [69], [70], [71], [72], [73].
4) Sequence Processing with LSTM: The CNN then feeds
the extracted features into the LSTM component for
further processing. We utilize LSTM to capture temporal dependencies and patterns over time, crucial for
accurately identifying typical attack patterns in DDoS
scenarios. The network can classify the data effectively
using the Sigmoid function, resulting in accurate classification outcomes [74], [75]. The LSTM’s training
on the CIC-DDoS2019 dataset enables it to recognize
temporal patterns associated with DDoS attacks, thus
improving the overall detection capabilities of the
IDS.

B. EXPERIMENTAL ENVIRONMENT
To implement and evaluate the proposed approach on the
CIC-DDoS2019 dataset, the experiment is conducted on a
processor Intel Core i5-13400, 10C (6P + 4E) / 16T, Pcore 2.5 / 4.6GHz, E-core 1.8 / 3.3GHz, 20MB; 32.0 GB
installed RAM; and 64-bit Ubuntu as Operating System.
Spyder Python (version 5.5) is used as an implementation tool
with some libraries such as Numpy (version 1.26.4), Pandas
(2.2.0), Scikit-learn (version 1.3), Keras (version 3.0.4), and
TensorFlow (version 2.15.0).

C. EVALUATION METRICS
We evaluated the HIDS-RPL framework using accuracy,
precision, recall, and the F1-score. This section begins
with an analysis of the four key parameters—True Positive
(TP), False Negative (FN), False Positive (FP), and True
Negative (TN)—used to derive metrics such as accuracy,
precision, recall, and the F1-score. True Positive refers to
the number of instances correctly classified as normal, while
False Negative indicates the rate at which normal data is
incorrectly categorized as an attack. False Positive represents
malicious instances incorrectly classified as normal, and True
Negative counts instances accurately classified as malicious.
Each of these evaluation metrics was calculated using
Equations 8, 9, 10, and 11.
Accuracy = TP + TN
TP + FP + TN + FN (8)
Recall = TP
FN + TP (9)

D. RESUL TS AND ANAL YSIS
The comparison of our suggested model with other models,
including CNN and LSTM, is displayed in Table 7. Table 7
shows that for the majority of attack types, the HIDS-RPL
model performed better in terms of accuracy and precision
scores than the conventional CNN and LSTM models. More
specifically, for DNS, NTP , and UDP attacks, the HIDS-RPL
model achieved high accuracy rates of 99.87%, 99.84%, and
98.98%, respectively. On the other hand, for the identical
attack types, the CNN model was able to classify benign
nodes with an accuracy of 86.80%, 99.49%, and 97.65%.
Additionally, the HIDS-RPL model outperformed the LSTM
model in terms of precision ratings for both DNS and
UDP attacks, scoring 79.79% and 76.05%, respectively.
In this way, and with the increasing interest in IoT devices,
these performance results highlight the effectiveness of the
proposed HIDS-RPL model in accurately detecting and
classifying different types of attacks.
1) ACCURACY PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS
Figure 6a shows the accuracy performance of our proposed
hybrid model compared to the Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) models
to detect DNS DDoS attacks. Indeed, results obtained on
the training and validation set demonstrate that the proposed
model maintains consistent accuracy and can achieve more
than 99.87% of the validation accuracy. As mentioned above,
it is evident that when we combine the spatial learning characteristics of CNNs with the temporal sequence capability of
LSTMs, we are able to outperform the performance of both
individual models (CNN and LSTM), and it appears to give
the hybrid model exceptional robustness in detecting DNS
DDoS attacks. Also, our proposed model exhibits increased
resistance to overfitting compared to the LSTM model alone,
as depicted in Figure 6b for Syn DDoS attack detection,
Figure 6c for NTP DDoS attack detection, and Figure 6e for
UDP DDoS attack detection.
Figure 6c illustrates a comparative evaluation of the
performance of the NTP DDoS attack detection models,
namely our proposed hybrid model, the model based only on
CNN, and the model based only on LSTM. Results obtained
after 50 training epochs demonstrate the accuracy for the
three architectures. From Figure 6c, it is clear that our proposed HIDS-RPL provides impressive outcomes for accuracy
compared to the CNN and LSTM models taken individually.
Additionally, deploying a combined CNN-LSTM model in
the context of the RPL protocol offers a favorable prospect for
detecting and identifying anomalies associated with DDoS
attacks.
According to the experiments and the observations,
we clearly identify that the performance of our proposed
model, HIDS-RPL, exhibits exceptional effectiveness in
detecting DDoS attacks, particularly in the sensitive context
of health. Further, in an intrusion detection system context,
our proposed model acts as the first line of defense to filter
and match attack traffic targeting the compromised node.
In this way, HIDS-RPL offers a promising solution for accurate detection of DDoS attacks in real-world environments.
2) LOSS RATE ANALYSIS
The comparative analysis of the loss functions for the
three models deployed for the detection of DDoS attacks is
depicted in Figure 7. During the first epochs, we can observe
clearly that our proposed model displays a steady decrease
in loss on both the training and validation sets. For example,
the loss values for the training set in Figure 7a at epoch 1 is
0.009, and at epoch 10 is 0.0014. This evolution suggests a
gradual convergence of our proposed model, characterized

by a consistent reduction in the loss. Furthermore, the CNN
model shows similar results but with higher loss values (e.g.,

demonstrates less linear initial convergence with higher

Overall, the experiments and the observations reveal the
performance of our proposed hybrid model, where the lower
loss values suggest a better fit to the training and validation
data, potentially indicating increased robustness in detecting
DDoS attacks. In fact, the subtle differences observed in
loss trajectories between the three models are related to the
combinatorial nature of hybrid architecture, which exploits
both spatial and temporal features to improve accuracy
in anomaly recognition as well as the deployment of the
HIDS-RPL procedure to detect malicious activities.
Figure 7c depicts the training and validation loss for
the three models dedicated to the detection of NTP DDoS
attacks, namely the proposed hybrid model HIDS-RPL,
the individual CNN model, and the individual LSTM
model. The proposed model exhibits a steady decrease in
training loss and validation loss across epochs, indicating
more efficient convergence compared to other models and
therefore demonstrating robust generalization. As mentioned
earlier, the hybrid model combines the advantages of spatial
perception of CNN and sequential understanding of LSTM,
thus enabling deep and sequential representation of data.
Ultimately, the individual CNN model also displays rapid
convergence with decreasing training and validation losses,
indicating effective adaptation to the data. However, its
primary reliance on spatial characterization may result in
a lower ability to capture complex sequential patterns.
The individual LSTM model, although demonstrating initial
convergence, exhibits notable divergence between training
and validation losses, suggesting potential for overfitting.
Its performance is particularly sensitive to the sequential
characteristics of the data, with a higher validation loss.
In this regard, experimental results obtained from Figure 7
show that in the context of the RPL protocol, our proposed
model provides effective detection of DDoS attacks by
exploiting the temporal and spatial characteristics of network
traffic. In fact, the ability to combine the spatial perception
of CNN and the sequential understanding of LSTM positions
them as a suitable solution for anomaly detection in an RPL
environment. Furthermore, the remarkable accuracy of our
proposed model provides further confidence in its ability to
significantly contribute to the security of emerging networks,
thereby strengthening defense mechanisms against DDoS
attacks.
Figure 7e illustrates the accuracies of the hybrid model
with the individual CNN and LSTM models for detecting
UDP DDoS attacks. Through experimental results, we can
observe that the training values of three models, executed
over 50 epochs, exhibit distinct characteristics. HIDSRPL model shows a refined convergence trajectory with
diminishing loss. This indicates that our hybrid model has a
deeper understanding of the complex spatiotemporal patterns
present in UDP DDoS attacks, surpassing the capabilities
of the individual CNN and LSTM models. In the context
of intrusion detection systems, particularly in healthcare
systems that use the RPL protocol, the significance of the
hybrid model becomes even more apparent. Malicious nodes
can exploit the RPL protocol to launch UDP DDoS attacks,
posing a significant threat to healthcare infrastructure.
Furthermore, Figure 8 depicted the confusion matrix
derived from the DNS attack, the NTP attack, the UDPLag
attack, and the UDP attack, with an overall accuracy of
99.87%, 99.84%, 99.80%, and 98.98%, respectively. To sum
up, in the healthcare field, where the secure availability of
systems is crucial for the proper functioning of online medical
services, our proposed model is positioned as a reliable
solution. Its ability to effectively discern malicious patterns
in network traffic provides enhanced protection against
DDoS attacks, potentially detrimental to the continuity of
healthcare services. In addition, integrating the hybrid model
into healthcare intrusion detection systems shows significant
promise, anticipating more accurate detection of DDoS
attacks and thereby helping to strengthen the resilience
and security of critical IT infrastructures for healthcare
services.

E. DISCUSSION
In our experiments, we demonstrate that our hybrid method,
which combines CNN and LSTM models, offers significant
improvements over existing methods, particularly in handling
the complex temporal dependencies found in IoMT network
traffic. The CNN component has outstanding ability in

FIGURE 8. Confusion matrix of the proposed model HIDS-RPL.
extracting spatial information from the input data, while the
LSTM exhibits excellent skill in capturing temporal patterns.
This combination makes our model highly effective in identifying complex attack patterns, including those encountered
in DDoS attacks. We emphasize that our hybrid approach
enhances detection accuracy, reaching a maximum accuracy
of 99.87%, a precision of 98.5%, a recall of 98.64%, and
an F1-score of 98.54%. This outperforms typical IDS methods, which frequently depend on single-model approaches.
Moreover, the fact that our model uses both CNN and LSTM
models makes it better at generalizing across different types
of attacks, especially those with small changes in time that
other methods often miss. Furthermore, Table 8 shows that
our proposed IDS is much better at both detecting threats and
maintaining balanced performance metrics when compared
to the most powerful methods currently available. Our
model, employing 20 carefully selected features, achieves an
accuracy of 99.87%, precision of 98.5%, recall of 98.64%,
and F1-score of 98.54%, reflecting its robust capability in
accurately identifying malicious activities. In contrast, prior
studies have varied significantly in feature complexity and
resultant performance. For example, [32] utilized 68 features
and reported high accuracy at 98.92% and precision reaching
99.92%. However, they reported fewer metrics, which limited
a comprehensive comparison across all performance areas.
Sharma et al. [36], who used a larger set of 115 features,
got a similar accuracy of 99.4%, but their precision and F1scores went up and down between 97% and 99.9%, which

TABLE 8. HIDS-RPL ’s result in comparison with the existing DL algorithms.
could mean they weren’t always able to spot certain types of
intrusions.
Other approaches, such as [30], operated with minimal
feature utilization (1 feature), yielding 96.59% accuracy but
lacking key insights into precision and recall. Al-Sarawi et al.
[34] presented a rule-based method that achieved broad
accuracy (90%–100%), but did not report on precision or
recall, which limited its comparability for nuanced intrusion
types. The IoTID20 dataset, employed in study [37], attained
an accuracy between 99.4% and 100% and consists of
450,000 examples of attacks classified into four categories:
Mirai, DoS, Scan, and MITM. The second dataset, CIRACIC-DoHBrw-2020, referenced in [40], comprises around
270,000 samples. It concentrates exclusively on malicious
Domain Name System (DNS) actions, highlighting the
identification of DNS over HTTPS (DoH) attacks. The
dataset consists of two primary categories of traffic: 20,000
samples of benign DoH traffic and 249,836 samples of
malicious DoH traffic.
The methodological limitations of the IoTID20 and the
CIRACIC-DoHBrw-2020 datasets become evident when
evaluated against the fidelity and scope of the CICDDoS2019 dataset. IoTID20, while containing 450,000
samples, employs 83 features derived from static network
snapshots and omits dynamic flow characteristics such as
bidirectional packet timing (e.g., inter-arrival times between
packets in both directions) and protocol-state transitions (e.g.,
changes in protocol behavior during communication sessions) critical for detecting multi-stage intrusions. Similarly,
CIRACIC-DoHBrw-2020, which is focused on DNS-overHTTPS threats, only uses 21 features and 270,000 samples,
which means it can only be used for specific attack vectors
and not for the wide range of traffic patterns seen in real
networks.
In contrast, CIC-DDoS2019 integrates 88 features and
50,063,123 records—including flow duration, inter-packet
intervals, and application-layer metadata—captured via
CICFlowMeter from live network interactions and real
testbeds. This facilitates a detailed examination of 12 specific DDoS types (e.g., SYN floods, HTTP-based attacks),
illustrating the multi-vector strategies common in actual
operations. Moreover, the exclusion of temporal variability
and ambient noise (e.g., device heterogeneity) in IoTID20
and CIRACIC-DoHBrw-2020 diminishes their potential to
evaluate detectors in realistic, noisy environments. These
limitations compromise the models’ ability to generalize by
favoring overfitting to idealized environments. The many
features, different types of attacks, and real traffic capture in
CIC-DDoS2019 fill in these gaps, making it a good standard
for testing detection frameworks against new threats.
As previously stated in this research, the CIC-DDoS2019
dataset comprises records of real-world traffic. The dataset
naturally organizes into distinct files, each focusing on a
different type of DDoS attack, such as DNS, UDP , NTP ,
and UDP-Lag. It was made by the Canadian Institute for
Cybersecurity to make managing its large dataset (about
50 million records) easier while keeping consistent feature
sets (that is, the same number and names of features for all
threats). Each threat type has a different file size, such as the
DNS threat being 2 GB, the NTP threat being 600 MB, and
the UDPLag threat being 155 MB. During the pre-processing
phase, each file was pre-processed independently (e.g., fixing
missing values, eliminating duplicates, and normalizing the
data), but the model was trained on an aggregated dataset
integrating all attack scenarios. In addition, we highlight
that the proposed IDS model, including its parameters,
remains consistent across all attack types; in other words, the
architecture itself does not change.
These results establish our IDS as a competitive and robust
solution within intrusion detection research, particularly
suited for complex network environments requiring high
reliability and precision. While it is acknowledged that the
performance of the proposed IDS may vary in different
scenarios due to factors such as hardware limitations, network configurations, and dataset characteristics, the model’s
strength lies in its adaptability to resource-constrained IoT
environments.
This adaptability is further reinforced by our proposed
framework, which not only enhances resilience against
diverse attack vectors but also ensures continual model evolution in response to emerging threats. Our proposed framework
manages evolving threats by focusing on low-level feature
representations, periodic retraining, anomaly detection, and
a modular deployment strategy. Through comprehensive
38426 VOLUME 13, 2025

A. Berguiga et al.: HIDS-RPL: A Hybrid DL-Based IDS for RPL in IoMT Network
data transformations and layered abstraction, the CNN
extracts spatial attributes, while the LSTM subsequently
captures long-range temporal dependencies. This design
lessens overreliance on narrow signatures, enabling the model
to respond more flexibly to novel adversarial behaviors.
In addition, we introduce a threshold variable τ , whose main
job is to find nodes that might be malicious. Furthermore,
the collaboration between fog nodes and cloud resources
permits more powerful computational resources to be leveraged for thorough analyses of suspicious traffic, thereby
allowing newly labeled events to refine the model’s decision
boundaries in subsequent training cycles. When unusual
traffic patterns arise, threshold-based anomaly detection
methods generate alerts and trigger deeper investigations.
Combining these strategies with the occasional tuning of
hyperparameters, including τ , promotes continual model
evolution and sustains detection efficacy under shifting IoMT
traffic profiles. Consequently, the system remains equipped
to handle emerging vulnerabilities and unseen exploits over
time.
Furthermore, our research illustrates a methodological
framework that may be adapted and refined for alternative
contexts, guaranteeing its applicability beyond the evaluated
configuration. Additionally, no prior research has utilized the
combination of CNN and LSTM specifically for intrusion
detection in the RPL protocol using the CIC-DDoS2019
dataset, underscoring the novelty and significance of our
contribution. In addition to this, the high scores achieved by
our model across accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score
underscore its effectiveness as a comprehensive solution for
detecting a broad range of attacks in various IoT and IoMT
applications while maintaining efficiency in minimizing false
positives.
VI. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS
Currently, RPL stands out as the dominant routing protocol
for 6LoWPAN-based IoT applications. Nevertheless, this
protocol faces a major security challenge in the form
of distributed denial of service attacks. Malicious nodes
can exploit control messages like DIO, DIS, and DAO to
orchestrate DDoS attacks within IoT applications, including
those in the domain of the internet of medical things. The
key objective of this study was to introduce an innovative
intrusion detection system exploiting deep learning models
for detecting intrusions in the IoMT environment. The
proposed system, named HIDS-RPL, was performed using
a combination of two models: CNN and LSTM. The HIDSRPL was trained and evaluated with the CIC-DDoS2019
dataset. The experimental results concluded that our hybrid
model, HIDS-RPL, outperformed the other conventional
CNN and LSTM models with an accuracy of 99.87%,
a precision rate of 98.5%, a recall rate of 98.64%, and an F1score value of 98.54%.
As per the future direction of this proposed work, different
imbalanced attacks will be included, as we intend to
increase the model’s range to encompass a larger variety
of cyber-attack categories, such as ransomware and insider
threats, in order to enhance detection capabilities in diverse
IoMT scenarios. Possible future improvements could also
rely on the threshold, τ . As previously stated, the threshold
τ , set at 500 milliseconds, was derived from the work of
Thulasiraman et al. [53] and validated under static network
conditions. This threshold is based on simulations conducted
using the Cooja network simulator and Wireshark tools with
mobile sensors moving at human walking speed (1–2 m/s).
This simulation environment mostly addresses low-mobility
conditions. While the value indicates effectiveness under
controlled conditions, scenarios characterized by higher
mobility or frequent topological changes could affect its
validity. Future research will examine τ across various
mobility models, incorporating increased speeds and varied
densities, to evaluate its flexibility and reliability.
Another important future direction is computational complexity, where the system must reduce both computational
and power requirements in order to improve data processing
efficiency and facilitate timely threat identification. Additionally, we aim to implement the proposed HIDS-RPL
architecture in a real Internet of Medical Things environment
using ContikiOS. This solution will allow us to analyze
numerous factors that affect the detection process, including
real-time limitations and network scalability.

ABDELWAHED BERGUIGA received the Engineering degree in computer science from
ISITCom, University of Sousse, Tunisia, in 2007,
the M.Sc. degree in computer science from
the Faculty of Sciences of Monastir (FSM),
University of Monastir, Tunisia, in 2009, and
the Ph.D. degree in computer science from the
National School of Computer Science (ENSI),
University of Manouba, Tunisia, in 2016. He is
currently an Assistant Professor at Jouf University,
Saudi Arabia. He is also a member of the PRINCE Research Laboratory,
ISITCom, University of Sousse. His current research interests include the IoT
security, wireless security, mobile wireless communication systems, artificial
intelligence, and next-generation networks.
AHLEM HARCHAY received the Engineering degree in computer science
from ISITCom, University of Sousse, Tunisia, in 2007, the M.Sc. degree
in computer science from the Faculty of Sciences of Monastir (FSM),
University of Monastir, Tunisia, in 2009, and the Ph.D. degree in computer
science from the National School of Computer Science (ENSI), University
of Manouba, Tunisia, in 2016. She is currently an Assistant Professor at Jouf
University, Saudi Arabia. She is also a member of the PRINCE Research
Laboratory, ISITCom, University of Sousse. Her current research interests
include machine learning, deep learning, and mobility in the Internet of
Things (IoT) fields.
AYMAN MASSAOUDI received the Engineering and M.Sc. degrees in
telecommunications and the Ph.D. degree in information and communication
technologies (ICT) from the Higher School of Communication of Tunis
(Sup’Com), University of Carthage, Tunisia, in 2008, 2009, and 2016,
respectively. He is currently a member of the Mediatron Research
Laboratory, Sup’Com, University of Carthage. He is also an Assistant
Professor at Gabes University, Tunisia. His current research interests include
communication systems, embedded systems, diversity techniques, massive
MIMO techniques, cognitive radio systems, UA Vs, 5G networks, and the
Internet of Things (IoT).
